title: The Listening Ice
passage:
Beneath the Straumfonna glacier, a thread of ordinary telecommunications fiber is doing the work of a thousand seismometers. Dr. Kel Ostrander of the Varden Polar Institute and his team lowered nearly four kilometers of the cable into a meltwater borehole two winters ago and then let it freeze into the ice. A laser pulse sent down the fiber scatters faintly off flaws in the glass; when the surrounding ice shifts, it stretches the cable by a few billionths of a meter and alters that scattered light. Reading those changes, the team can fix the position of each tiny fracture as the glacier grinds downhill, taking a fresh reading every ten meters along the whole length of the cable. Ostrander is quick to add that the method shows only where the ice is cracking, not how much water is pooling beneath it; for that, he says, the boreholes still have to be drilled and sampled by hand.
— Marlo Quist, science desk

questions.prompt: According to the text, what does the fiber-optic cable allow Ostrander's team to do?

options.text: Measure how much meltwater is collecting in the ice beneath the moving glacier.
Locate where the ice is fracturing as the glacier moves, by sensing tiny stretches in the cable.
Do away with the boreholes that would otherwise be drilled into the glacier by hand.
Send laser pulses strong enough to melt a channel through the surrounding ice.

questions.rationale: TYPE-001 direct detail (paraphrase_one_sentence). The text states that reading the changes in the scattered light lets the team 'fix the position of each tiny fracture as the glacier grinds downhill', and that the shifting ice 'stretches the cable by a few billionths of a meter'; B paraphrases exactly that. A = contradiction/scope_error: the passage explicitly rules it out ('not how much water is pooling beneath it'). C = contradiction/quantifier_upgrade: the text says the boreholes 'still have to be drilled and sampled by hand', so the cable does not 'do away with' them. D = surface_word_match: it recycles 'laser pulse', but the passage says the pulse 'scatters faintly off flaws in the glass', not that it melts ice; melting is nowhere claimed.

fictional_source.byline: Marlo Quist, invented science journalist; research attributed to invented Dr. Kel Ostrander
